1: Bišnat ʾaḥat lᵉḵôreš — 'In the first year of Cyrus': the regnal-year formula anchors the return in Persian administrative time. Cyrus II conquered Babylon in 539 BCE; his first regnal year by the Babylonian system was 538 BCE. The explanatory clause 'so that the word of YHWH through Jeremiah might be fulfilled' (lᵉḵallôt dᵉḇar-YHWH mippî yirmᵉyāhû) cites Jer 25:11-12 and 29:10 (70 years of exile); the verb klh in Piel = to complete/bring to completion, framing the return as divine word accomplishing its purpose. The key verb of the verse is hēʿîr YHWH ʾet-rûaḥ kôreš: 'YHWH stirred up the spirit of Cyrus.' ʿûr Hiphil = to rouse, arouse from sleep or inertia; the same verb describes YHWH rousing Cyrus in Isa 41:2 and 45:13. The rûaḥ (spirit/inner disposition) of Cyrus is the object of YHWH's action — not external coercion but internal motivation. The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE) records Cyrus attributing his mandate to Marduk rather than YHWH; the author of Ezra reads the same historical event as YHWH's doing, consistent with Isa 44:28-45:1 (YHWH names Cyrus 'my shepherd' and 'his anointed').
2: Cyrus's proclamation (kōh ʾāmar kôreš = 'thus says Cyrus') follows the standard ancient Near Eastern royal proclamation formula, the same kōh ʾāmar used for prophetic oracle delivery — the king speaks with the same grammatical authority as a prophet. The content claim is remarkable: kol-mamᵉlᵉḵôt hāʾāreṣ nātan-lî YHWH ʾĕlōhê haššāmāyim — 'YHWH the God of heaven has given me all the kingdoms of the earth.' The title ʾĕlōhê haššāmāyim (God of heaven) is the standard late-Biblical and Persian-era designation for YHWH (appears in Neh 1:4-5; Dan 2:18-19; 2 Chr 36:23), reflecting the lingua franca of Persian imperial theology where the deity of a subject people is acknowledged as a cosmic power. Whether Cyrus actually used this title or the editor inserted it is debated; the Cyrus Cylinder uses 'Marduk, lord of the gods' in the same position. The commission wayhûʾ pāqad ʿālay ('he has charged/commissioned me') uses pāqad (to appoint, visit, commission) — the same verb used for YHWH's commissioning of Moses (Ex 3:16).
3: The invitation formula mî-ḇāḵem mikkol-ʿammô ('whoever among you from all his people') is deliberately inclusive — 'whoever' (mî = anyone) from among all YHWH's people, not limited to the Judahites. The blessing formula yᵉhî ʾĕlōhāyw ʿimmô ('may his God be with him') echoes the patriarchal covenant-presence formula (Gen 28:15 'I will be with you'; Deut 20:4 'YHWH your God is with you'). The command to go up (yaʿal) to Jerusalem uses the standard biblical verb for pilgrimage and return to the land — ʿālāh (to go up) is always the directional term for movement toward Jerusalem, whether from Egypt (Ex 1:10; 17:3), from the wilderness, or from Babylon. The description of Jerusalem as 'which is in Judah' (ʾăšer bîhûdāh) is a territorial identification for Persians unfamiliar with the specific city's location within the administrative province of Yehud.
4: The provision formula for those who remain (kol-hannišʾār mikkol-hamᵉqōmôt ʾăšer hûʾ gār-šām) — 'every survivor, wherever he sojourns' — uses nišʾār (the remaining/surviving ones), a term that in post-exilic context carries the weight of the prophetic remnant (šᵉʾērît, e.g., Isa 10:20-22; Mic 5:7). The provision list (silver, gold, goods, livestock, freewill offerings) mirrors the Exodus provisions: in Ex 12:35-36 the Israelites ask their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold and clothing; here Persian neighbors assist the returning exiles. The parallel is deliberate: Ezra's theology of return patterns the new exodus on the Mosaic original. nᵉdāḇōt (freewill offerings) is the term for voluntary cultic gifts (Lev 7:16; Deut 12:6); the designation signals that the proceeds for Jerusalem will be channeled into the cult.
5: The response formula wayyāqûmû ('they rose up') describes the mobilization of the returnees. The leadership list — heads of fathers' houses of Judah and Benjamin, priests and Levites — represents the tripartite structure of the post-exilic community: tribal/lay leadership (Judah + Benjamin), priestly leadership, and Levitical support. The key phrase is kōl ʾăšer hēʿîr hāʾĕlōhîm ʾet-rûḥô lāʿălôt ('everyone whose spirit God had stirred up to go up'): the same ʿûr + rûaḥ construction from v1 is applied to the people — just as YHWH stirred Cyrus's spirit to make the decree, so YHWH stirs each individual returnee's spirit to respond. The divine arousal creates a coherent movement: the sovereign stirs the pagan king, and the same verb stirs the covenant people. The verb lāʿălôt (to go up) = pilgrimage-return vocabulary.
6: The neighbors' assistance — kol-sᵉḇîḇōtêhem ḥizzᵉqû ḇîḏêhem ('all their neighbors strengthened their hands') — uses the idiom ḥizzēq yāḏ (to strengthen the hand = to assist, support), which appears in Neh 2:18 for the community's response to Nehemiah's report. The list of gifts repeats the Exodus provision pattern from v4, adding uḇimigdānôt (precious/choice things, from megādānôt = precious objects, cf. Gen 24:53 for bridal gifts). The phrase lᵉḇad ʿal kol-mitnaddēḇ ('besides all that was voluntarily offered') emphasizes that the community gifts exceeded the official Persian provision — a double generosity, reminiscent of the wilderness tabernacle construction where the community gave so much Moses had to stop them (Ex 36:3-7).
7: The temple vessels (kᵉlê bêt-YHWH) that Nebuchadnezzar had taken from Jerusalem (2 Kgs 24:13; 2 Chr 36:7,10,18) and placed in the temple of his gods (bêt ʾĕlōhāyw) are now returned. The restoration of the cult vessels is a theological reversal: what conquest had transferred to the victor's temple, covenant faithfulness now restores to the original sanctuary. The irony of the term bêt ʾĕlōhāyw (house of his gods) — a pagan temple that temporarily held YHWH's sacred vessels — echoes Dan 1:2 (Nebuchadnezzar places the Jerusalem vessels 'in the house of his god'). The return of the vessels is a visible covenant restoration: the objects of YHWH's cult are returning to their proper home.
8: Mîṯrᵉdāt haggizzāḇār — Mithredath the treasurer. The name Mithredath is Iranian, from Mithra (the Persian solar deity) + dāta (given) = 'given by Mithra'; it appears as a Persian court name in Ezra 4:7 as well. Haggizzāḇār is a Babylonian/Persian loanword (ganzabara = 'guardian of the treasury') — a technical administrative title for the imperial treasurer. The vessels are counted (yispᵉrēm = enumerate/count) and transferred to Sheshbazzar. Šᵉšbaṣṣar nᵉśîʾ yᵉhûdāh — Sheshbazzar the prince of Judah. Nāśîʾ is the hereditary/honorific term for tribal/dynastic prince (cf. Ezek 44-48 for the Davidic nāśîʾ); here it distinguishes Sheshbazzar's status as the Davidic representative from his role as Persian-appointed pāḥāh (governor, Ezra 5:14). Most scholars identify Sheshbazzar as Shenazzar, son of Jehoiachin (1 Chr 3:18), making him a Davidic prince and the uncle of Zerubbabel.
9: The vessel inventory begins with ʾagarṭᵉlê (basins/bowls): the Hebrew word is a loanword possibly from Persian or Babylonian — used only here in the OT. The thirty gold basins (ʾagarṭᵉlê zāhāḇ šᵉlōšîm) are large cultic vessels for temple service. One thousand silver basins (ʾagarṭᵉlê kesep ʾelep) follow. Maḥălāpîm ʾešrîm-wᵉtišʿāh — 29 knives/censers; maḥălāpîm may be a hapax (unique usage) possibly related to ḥālap (to pass through, change) — instruments for changing/rotating sacrificial items, or possibly for trimming wicks; the exact function is uncertain. The inventory functions both as administrative record and as theological marker: the number of vessels being returned corresponds to the original items taken, signaling complete restitution.
10: Kᵉfôrê zāhāḇ šᵉlōšîm — thirty gold bowls. Kāpôr (bowl/basin) is a cultic vessel term that appears in the tabernacle/temple inventory (Ex 25:29; Num 7:13-86 for the dedication offerings). The 'silver bowls of a second kind' (kᵉfôrê kesep mišnîm) — mišnîm = of a second grade/type, implying the gold bowls are the superior grade and silver are second-tier. Four hundred and ten silver bowls second-grade is a large number. The thousand 'other vessels' (kēlîm ʾăḥērîm ʾālep) is a catch-all category at the end — the inventory has been exhaustive in the named categories but the residual thousand vessels resist exact classification. This matches standard ancient Near Eastern temple inventory practice, which would list named vessels individually and group miscellaneous items at the end.
11: The grand total: kol-kēlîm lāzāhāḇ wᵉlakesep ḥămiššet ʾălāpîm wᵉʾarbaʿ mēʾôt = 5,400 vessels of gold and silver. A mathematical problem is well-known: the specific items listed in vv9-10 sum to considerably less than 5,400 (the Septuagint and 1 Esdras have different numbers for individual items). The discrepancy may reflect lost text, rounding, or the inclusion of items not individually enumerated. The round-number summary 5,400 may function as the authoritative total in the official Persian record, with vv9-10 being selective highlights. Šᵉšbaṣṣar heʿĕlāh ʾet-hakkōl bᵉhaʿălôt haggōlāh mibbāḇel lîrûšālaim — Sheshbazzar brought all of it up when the exiles came up from Babylon to Jerusalem. The verb heʿĕlāh (brought up) echoes the earlier yaʿal (go up) of the pilgrimage-return vocabulary: the vessels go up to Jerusalem just as the people do — the cult objects participate in the exodus-return with the community.
